    18→
    19→## Critical UX / Offline-First Fixes (Pre-Closed-Testing)
    20→
    21→[x] Issue 1 — Parties list layout break on long names
    22→    → PartyCard.tsx: added overflow-hidden + min-w-0 to middle section and badge row; flex-shrink-0 on badge; fixed-width w-[86px] on balance block; truncate on amount text. Badge can no longer push into action buttons.
    23→
    24→[x] Issue 2 — App version mismatch in Settings
    25→    → SettingsView.tsx: added Capacitor App.getInfo() call on mount (native platforms only); stores result in displayVersion state; falls back to APP_VERSION constant on web. Settings now always reflects the actual installed Android build version.
    26→
    27→[x] Issue 3 — Offline-first not truly offline-first
    28→    → DataContext.tsx: throttleTime reduced from 1000ms → 0 so IndexedDB writes happen immediately after every cache change (no 1-second window where a process-kill would lose the entry).
    29→    → useOptimisticMutation.ts: all three mutationFns (add/update/delete) now check navigator.onLine first. When offline: entry is routed through SyncQueueService (localStorage-backed, survives restarts) and returns success immediately with a stable offlineId — no server call, no onError rollback. Entries are instantly visible, survive app restart before reconnect, and reconcile via the sync queue when back online.
    30→
    31→[x] Issue 4 — Admin panel settings not applied on first login (subscription flicker)
    32→    → SubscriptionContext.tsx: added globalConfigLoaded state that is set to true on first globalConfig onSnapshot (both success and error paths), with a 4-second safety timeout as fallback. The public loading flag now gates on BOTH subscriptionLoaded AND globalConfigLoaded, so the dashboard never renders until remote config is resolved.
    33→    → SettingsView.tsx: subscription tab filter now treats null globalConfig as 'free' mode (safe-by-default). User never sees the subscription section even for a single frame if it should be hidden.
    34→
    35→[x] Issue 5 — AI section save button loading state affects all buttons
    36→    → CommandModal.tsx: replaced single loading boolean with two separate states: aiLoading (for send/AI processing) and savingMsgIds (Set<string> keyed by message.id for save operations). Each "Save All" button now independently shows its own spinner; clicking one never disables unrelated save buttons.
    37→
    38→[x] Issue 6 — Bulk import (sale/purchase) always shows 5 product fields regardless of CSV
    39→    → BulkImportView.tsx: added activeSchema state. parseFile now detects actual item slot count from uploaded CSV headers via detectMaxItemSlot() and always calls buildDynamicSchema(type, Math.max(detected, 1)) to build the schema from the real file — not the fixed ITEM_SLOTS=5 constant. The map step UI, mapped counter, buildPreview, and retryAiMapping all now use activeSchema instead of SCHEMA[type]. If a CSV has 10 items, all 10 slots appear; if it has 1, only 1 appears.
    40→
    41→[x] Issue 7 — Reports, Vehicles, VehicleDetailView flickering/reloading every second
    42→    → Root cause: useEffect deps used the full `user` and `vehicle` objects. Firebase auth can reissue the user object reference on every auth tick, making React treat it as a new dependency and re-fire the load effect repeatedly.
    43→    → ReportsView.tsx: changed `}, [user])` → `}, [user.uid])`.
    44→    → VehiclesView.tsx: changed `}, [user])` → `}, [user.uid])`.
    45→    → VehicleDetailView.tsx: changed `}, [vehicle, user])` → `}, [vehicle.id, user.uid])` — both primitives, stable across re-renders.
    46→
    47→[x] Issue 8 — React error #300 in item detail view and customer detail view
    48→    → Root cause: Both InventoryView.tsx and PartiesView.tsx had a `useCallback` hook called INSIDE the JSX return statement (as a Virtuoso `itemContent` prop), but AFTER conditional early returns (selectedInvItem / selectedParty / loading). This violated the Rules of Hooks — React was calling a different number of hooks depending on which branch was taken, producing "Minified React error #300" in the production Android build.
    49→    → Fix: Extracted the inline `useCallback` from inside JSX to a named constant defined BEFORE any early returns in both files:
    50→      • InventoryView.tsx: `renderInventoryRow` hoisted above `if (selectedInvItem) { ... }` at line 224.
    51→      • PartiesView.tsx: `renderPartyRow` hoisted above `if (loading)` and `if (selectedParty)` early returns.
    52→    → Virtuoso `itemContent` now references the stable named callback by identifier instead of calling a hook inline.
    53→
    54→[x] Issue 9 — Loading spinners on every screen for returning users (cache pre-warm)
    55→    → Root cause: `PersistQueryClientProvider` restores the IndexedDB cache asynchronously after React mounts. On a warm device, Firebase Auth + Firestore settings can resolve before the IDB rehydration finishes. This caused a ~100-200ms window where all queries showed `isLoading: true` (no data in memory yet) and every view rendered its skeleton/spinner, then data popped in.
    56→    → Fix: imported `useIsRestoring` from `@tanstack/react-query` in App.tsx. Added `isRestoringCache` to the existing LoadingView gate:
    57→      `if (!settingsLoaded || roleLoading || subscriptionLoading || isRestoringCache) return <LoadingView />;`
    58→    → Result: the app now holds at the branded LoadingView for the extra ~50-200ms it takes to restore the cache. Once `isRestoringCache` flips to false, ALL screens open instantly with data already populated — zero per-view spinners for returning users. First-time users (empty cache) see restore complete in <10ms — no perceptible delay.
    59→
    60→[x] Issue 10 — React error #300 on ledger order click (LedgerView) + proactive sweep
    61→    → LedgerView.tsx: same root cause — `useCallback` inside Virtuoso `itemContent` JSX at line 677, called AFTER `if (selectedDetail)` early return at line 478. Hoisted `renderLedgerRow` useCallback above the early return; replaced inline `itemContent={useCallback(...)}` with `itemContent={renderLedgerRow}`.
    62→    → Proactive sweep of all remaining views: found TransactionsView.tsx had the same violation at line 448 (inline useCallback in itemContent after `if (selectedDetail)` early return at line 333). Fixed identically — hoisted `renderTransactionRow` before the early return.
    63→    → Final grep confirms ZERO remaining `itemContent={useCallback(` patterns in the entire codebase. All Virtuoso lists now use hoisted named callbacks.
    64→
    65→[x] Issue 11 — Comprehensive hook violation sweep + Developer Console page-inside-page
    66→    → Hook sweep: ran `={useCallback(|={useMemo(` grep across all .tsx files — zero matches remain. All views confirmed clean.
    67→    → Also verified ExpensesView, WasteView, VehiclesView, PendingView individually — none use Virtuoso itemContent + early return pattern.
    68→    → SettingsView.tsx "page inside page" fix: when activeSection === 'developer', SettingsView was rendering CrashLogsView inside its own sub-page layout (which has its own ← Settings header). CrashLogsView ALSO has its own ← Crash Logs header — resulting in two stacked headers.
    69→      Fix: added a dedicated `if (activeSection === 'developer')` branch BEFORE the generic sub-page return that renders CrashLogsView directly inside a plain h-full container (no SettingsView header). Removed the now-unreachable duplicate developer block from inside the sub-page content div.
    70→
    71→[x] Issue 12 — "Saving…" button frozen forever on ALL saves (Sale, Purchase, Payment, Expense, Vehicle, Onboarding step 3)
    72→    → Root cause: Firestore's `persistentLocalCache` with `persistentSingleTabManager({ forceOwnership: true })` writes to IndexedDB for local persistence. On Android WebView under memory pressure (confirmed by BAD ALLOC GPU errors in device logs), the IndexedDB lock can be unacquirable — causing `batch.commit()` / `setDoc()` Promises to **never resolve AND never reject**. Since `setLoading(true)` / `setSaving(true)` is set before the hanging `await`, and `finally { setLoading(false) }` never runs, the button is frozen on "Saving…" indefinitely.
    73→    → Fix 1 — `src/services/api.ts`: Added `withWriteTimeout<T>(promise)` helper that races any write Promise against a hard 15-second deadline. If the deadline fires first, rejects with `{ code: 'WRITE_TIMEOUT' }`. Applied to ALL write operations: `add` (addDoc), `update` (updateDoc), `delete` (deleteDoc), `batchSave` (batch.commit), `settings.save` (setDoc), `updateSettings` (setDoc).
    74→    → Fix 2 — `src/components/modals/ManualEntryModal.tsx`: Wrapped the online `ApiService.batchSave()` call in an inner try/catch. On `WRITE_TIMEOUT`, iterates `batchOps` and queues each operation into `SyncQueueService` (durable localStorage queue), then shows "Saved offline — will sync when online" toast, closes modal, calls `onSuccess`. Data is never lost — the sync queue replays all ops on next healthy connection. Non-timeout errors still bubble to the outer catch.
    75→    → Fix 3 — `src/components/auth/OnboardingView.tsx`: Changed `saveFirmProfile` catch block to return `true` (allow wizard to advance) instead of `false` (block forever) on network/timeout errors. Shows a contextual warning toast: timeout → "Save timed out — profile will sync when connection improves", other errors → "Could not save profile — edit it later in Settings". Only hard validation failures (missing firm name, not signed in) still return `false` and block progression.
    76→    → TypeScript: zero errors after all three changes (`npx tsc --noEmit` clean).
    77→
    78→[x] Issue 12 — ROOT FIX: Removed Firestore persistentLocalCache (IndexedDB bottleneck)
    79→    → Root cause (deeper): persistentLocalCache routes EVERY Firestore write through IndexedDB first.
    80→      This layer is the source of the hang — on Android WebView under memory pressure the IndexedDB
    81→      lock is unacquirable, so batch.commit()/setDoc() Promises hang forever, never resolving or rejecting.
    82→    → Root fix: src/config/firebase.ts — switched from `persistentLocalCache({ tabManager: persistentSingleTabManager({ forceOwnership: true }) })` to `memoryLocalCache()`.
    83→      Firestore writes now go directly to the Firestore network with no IndexedDB intermediary.
    84→      The hang condition cannot occur with memoryLocalCache.
    85→    → Why it is safe (app has its own complete offline stack, making Firestore's built-in cache redundant):
    86→        • TanStack Query v5 + idb-keyval (queryPersistStorage.ts) → caches all business data reads in the app's own IDB store (7-day persistence). Cold-start while offline still serves full dataset.
    87→        • SyncQueueService (syncQueue.ts) → durable localStorage queue for offline writes. All creates/updates/deletes while offline are queued and replayed on reconnect.
    88→        • useOptimisticMutation + ManualEntryModal → UI reflects writes immediately regardless of network state.
    89→        • The 15-second WRITE_TIMEOUT guard in api.ts (from Issue 12 fix) provides a final safety net for any transient network issues.
    90→    → TypeScript: zero errors. App reloaded via HMR with no console errors.
    91→

1. **ManualEntryModal.tsx** — `getIDForEntry()` called in `useEffect` on form open → increments counter every open

**Fix strategy:** Add `peekNextID()` (shows next number without incrementing) and `confirmID()` (advances counter on actual save). Replace all form-open calls with `peekNextID`, call `confirmID` only on save.

Zero TypeScript errors, app running clean. All fixes are live. Here's a summary of exactly what was changed and why:

---

**Root cause:** `generatePrefixedID()` increments the counter and saves it to localStorage immediately — even when the form is just opened for preview. So every open-and-close wasted a number.

**3 problem spots fixed:**

| Location | Problem | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `ManualEntryModal.tsx` | `getIDForEntry()` called in `useEffect` on **every form open** | Now uses `peekNextID()` on open, `confirmID()` on save |
| `POSBillingView.tsx` | `getIDForEntry('sell')` in `useState()` — fires on **every POS screen mount** | Now uses `peekNextID('sales')` on mount, `confirmID()` in `persist()` |
| `TransactionForms.tsx` | `getIDForEntry(t)` called on **every Received/Paid toggle click** | Now uses `peekNextID()` which reads without touching the counter |

**How it works now:**
- **Open form** → `peekNextID()` shows the next number (counter + 1) without touching the counter. Open/close 100 times — counter stays put.
- **Save** → `confirmID()` advances the counter to the number that was actually used (handles both the auto-filled value and any custom number the user typed).
- **Custom number** → if user types "S-500", `confirmID` ensures the next auto-ID is "S-501".

Now I have the complete picture of the bug.

**Root cause:** Factory reset deletes Firestore documents, but then `window.location.reload()` fires and the app's TanStack Query persister immediately **restores the old data from IndexedDB** back into memory — before Firestore fetch completes. The stale data appears. When the user tries to edit, it fails because the underlying Firestore document is already gone, causing the UI to crash and drop everything.

**The fix:** Before reloading, the reset must clear: (1) IndexedDB React Query cache, (2) localStorage (sync queue, ID counters, drafts), (3) sessionStorage — so the reload starts completely fresh.

Here's exactly what was happening and what's fixed:

**What was broken:**

The factory reset deleted all Firestore documents correctly, then called `window.location.reload()`. But on reload, the app's offline cache (IndexedDB) kicked in *before* Firestore responded — restoring all the deleted data back into memory. The user saw the old data. When they tried to edit it, Firestore threw an error (the document was already deleted), which crashed the UI and wiped the visible data — making it look like everything disappeared *because* of the edit, not because of the reset.

**What's fixed:**

After Firestore deletion, the reset now also wipes all three local storage layers **before** reloading:

1. **IndexedDB** (`shopkeeper-cache` / `rq-cache-v1`) — the TanStack Query offline cache. This was the main culprit — the old data was coming from here.
2. **localStorage** — sync queue, ID counters, draft saves, all app state.
3. **sessionStorage** — any session-scoped state.

The reload now starts with a genuinely empty slate — Firestore is empty, caches are empty, so nothing stale can reappear.

### 1. Google Login (`signInWithGoogle`)
The Google login flow is primarily handled in `src/context/AuthContext.tsx` via the `loginWithGoogle` function. It supports both native (Capacitor) and web environments:

*   **Native (Android/iOS):** Uses the `@codetrix-studio/capacitor-google-auth` plugin. It retrieves an `idToken` from the plugin and then uses Firebase's `signInWithCredential` to authenticate with the backend.
*   **Web:** Uses Firebase's **`signInWithPopup`**. There is no evidence of `signInWithRedirect` being used in the main authentication flow.
*   **OAuth Scopes:** 
    *   For the main login, the requested scopes are `["profile", "email"]` as configured in `capacitor.config.ts` (lines 14-15).
    *   The `prompt: 'select_account'` parameter is used to force the account picker.

### 2. Google Drive Backup
The Google Drive backup functionality is implemented in `src/services/googleDriveBackupService.ts`.

*   **Implementation:** It uses the Google Drive API v3.
*   **OAuth Scopes:** It requests the specific scope `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.file` (line 5). This scope allows the app to see and manage only the files that it has created.
*   **Process:**
    1.  It calls `signInWithPopup` with a `GoogleAuthProvider` that has the Drive scope added (lines 9-13).
    2.  It obtains an `accessToken` from the resulting credential.
    3.  It searches for an existing backup file with a specific naming convention (`ShopKeeper_Backup_{email}_{date}.json`) and either creates a new file (POST) or updates the existing one (PATCH).
*   **Entry Point:** The service is triggered from the UI in `src/components/settings/SettingsDataZone.tsx` (line 95).

### 3. Google Contacts
The codebase **does not** appear to have a direct integration with the Google Contacts API (e.g., People API). Instead, contact management is handled through device-level integrations in `src/services/contactPickerService.ts`:

*   **Native (Capacitor):** Uses `@capacitor-community/contacts` to bulk-load contacts from the physical device's contact list (line 16).
*   **Web (Chrome/Android):** Uses the browser's native **Web Contact Picker API** (`navigator.contacts.select`) to allow users to select contacts from their device (line 83).
*   **Usage:** These contacts are cached locally and used for suggestions in forms (e.g., adding a new party/customer) within `src/components/modals/manual/SimpleForms.tsx`.

### Summary of OAuth Scopes
| Feature | Scopes Requested |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Authentication** | `profile`, `email` |
| **Google Drive Backup** | `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.file` |
| **Contacts** | None (Uses Device/Browser API, not Google OAuth Scopes) |

### Key Files
*   `src/context/AuthContext.tsx`: Main login logic.
*   `src/services/googleDriveBackupService.ts`: Google Drive integration.
*   `src/services/contactPickerService.ts`: Device-level contact picking.
*   `capacitor.config.ts`: Configuration for native Google Auth plugin.

Zero TypeScript errors. Here's a summary of exactly what changed and what you need to do on the Google side to activate it:

---

**What was built:**

| | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| **Google Drive backup** | Called `signInWithPopup` with `prompt: 'consent'` every time → blank page / sessionStorage error | Reuses the OAuth token stored at login — **no popup at all** during backup |
| **Google Contacts** | Web Contact Picker API (manual button, often unavailable) | Google People API auto-loads contacts silently as soon as the party form opens, if you're signed in with Google |
| **Login scopes** | Only `profile` + `email` | Now also requests Drive + Contacts permission in the same login popup |
| **Token lifetime** | Not stored | Stored in localStorage for 1 hour; Drive backup uses it directly |

**Flow after your next login:**
1. Tap "Sign in with Google" → Google shows one permission screen asking for Contacts + Drive access
2. You approve → you're logged in AND the token is saved
3. Open "Add Party" → contacts auto-load from Google Contacts in the background (no button needed)
4. Tap "Backup to Drive" → uses the saved token, no popup opens

---

**⚠️ One required step on Google Cloud Console:**

The Google People API (for contacts) must be enabled on your Firebase project. Do this once:

1. Go to [console.cloud.google.com](https://console.cloud.google.com) → select your Firebase project
2. **APIs & Services → Library** → search **"People API"** → Enable it
3. Also confirm **"Google Drive API"** is enabled (same place)

Without this, the contacts call will return a 403 and fall back silently (existing party data is unaffected). The Drive backup already works if Drive API is enabled.

Zero TypeScript errors. Here's what changed and how it works across all three paths:

---

**What was built — Party tags (`P-0001`, `P-0002` …) for every add path:**

| Path | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual add** (Add Party form) | ✅ Already had `P-XXXX` | Now also syncs the shared counter on save |
| **AI / voice** (Command Modal) | ❌ No tag assigned | ✅ Gets the next sequential tag automatically |
| **Bulk CSV import** | ❌ No tag assigned | ✅ Fetches existing parties, seeds counter, assigns tags in sequence |

**How the counter stays consistent across all paths:**

1. When any of the three UIs load, they call `seedPartyCounter()` with all existing `party_code` values from Firestore — so the counter always starts from the highest number already used
2. When saving, each path calls `generatePartyCode()` (increments) or `confirmPartyCode()` (catches up)
3. All three share the same module-level counter in memory, so within a session they'll never generate the same code twice even if you mix entry methods

**Example flow:**
- You have P-0001 to P-0010 in Firestore
- You open Add Party → shows P-0011 in the preview
- You ask AI to "add supplier Ram Trading" → saves with P-0012
- You bulk import 5 parties → they get P-0013 through P-0017

Here's what was wrong and what was fixed:

**Root cause — detached element bug in the hook (Dashboard + BulkImport)**

`useScrollMemory` saved scroll position in the `useEffect` cleanup by reading `el.scrollTop` — but by the time React fires the cleanup, the element has already been removed from the DOM. Chrome/WebView returns `0` for `scrollTop` on a detached element, so every navigation silently wrote `0` to sessionStorage, wiping the real position.

**Fix (`useScrollMemory.ts`):** A `capturedScrollTop` local variable (inside the effect closure) is updated on every `scroll` event — no throttle delay. The cleanup now saves this captured value instead of touching `el.scrollTop`. The throttled sessionStorage write is kept as a crash-resilience write, but the final save no longer depends on the DOM.

**Settings sub-sections — scroll memory was simply missing**

The content div at line 345 had no `ref` at all. All six sub-sections (Profile, General, Invoice, Lists, Security, Data…) shared one plain non-tracked div.

**Fix (`SettingsView.tsx`):** Added `subScrollRef = useScrollMemory('settings-sub-${activeSection}')` at the component level. When `activeSection` changes, the hook's key-change path saves the outgoing section's scroll position (element still attached in `useLayoutEffect` timing) and restores the incoming section's. The ref is now attached to the content div.

---

**Why "Daily Data Archive" worked but everything else didn't**

Daily Data Archive calls `onNavigate('daily-snapshots')` — that switches the entire `activeTab`, unmounting and remounting `SettingsView`. The hook's normal lifecycle (cleanup on unmount → save) fired correctly for that case.

All other sub-sections use `setActiveSection(id)` — the component **stays mounted**, only the rendered div swaps. `useEffect` cleanup never fires, so nothing was ever saved to sessionStorage. On the way back, `useLayoutEffect` didn't re-fire either (key `'settings-menu'` never changed).

**Three changes made to `SettingsView.tsx`:**

1. **`handleSectionClick(id)`** — wraps every section button. Reads `menuScrollRef.current.scrollTop` and saves it to sessionStorage *before* calling `setActiveSection`, while the element is still in the DOM.

2. **`handleBackToMenu`** — now saves `subScrollRef.current.scrollTop` for the current sub-section *before* transitioning back, so returning to a sub-section also restores where you were.

3. **`useLayoutEffect([activeSection])`** — when `activeSection` flips back to `'menu'`, manually restores the saved scroll position with the same RAF + 120ms retry strategy the hook uses, since the hook itself won't re-fire for a same-component conditional re-render.

Messages:
  - The record numbers (S-101, P-101, REC-101, etc.) in the Shopledger application are generated using a hybrid strategy that combines in-memory counters, local storage persistence, and Firestore-based seeding to ensure uniqueness and continuity across sessions and devices.

### 1. ID Generator Logic (`src/utils/idGenerator.ts`)
The core logic resides in `src/utils/idGenerator.ts`. It uses a prefix-based mapping for different record types:
*   **Sales:** `S-`
*   **Purchases:** `P-`
*   **Customers:** `C-`
*   **Suppliers:** `V-`
*   **Inventory:** `I-`
*   **Receipts:** `REC-`
*   **Payments:** `PAY-`
*   **Waste:** `W-`

**Counter Mechanism:**
*   **Default Start:** All counters default to `100` (so the first record is `101`).
*   **In-Memory State:** `_memCounters` holds the current session's highest used number.
*   **Persistence:** Counters are synced to `localStorage` (keyed by user UID) so they persist across refreshes.
*   **Firestore Seeding (`seedCountersFromFirestore`):** This is the "source of truth" mechanism. When records are fetched from Firestore (e.g., on app load or modal open), the `idGenerator` extracts the numeric portion of existing IDs and jumps the counter to the maximum value found. This prevents collisions if the same user logs in from a new device.

### 2. Usage in Transaction and Order Forms
The ID generation is integrated into the UI through two main patterns: **Peeking** and **Confirming**.

#### **Manual Entry Modal (`src/components/modals/ManualEntryModal.tsx`)**
*   **Initialisation:** When the modal opens for a new entry, it calls `peekNextID(category)` to show the user what the next ID *will be* without incrementing the counter yet (Lines 112-118).
*   **Seeding:** The modal uses `useParties`, `useInventory`, and `useLedger` hooks (from `DataContext.tsx`) to get cached data. It calls `seedPartyCounter` (Line 66) to ensure the `P-XXXX` party codes are in sync with existing records.
*   **Submission (`handleSubmit`):**
    *   If the user hasn't manually edited the ID, it calls `getIDForEntry(type)` to get the next ID and increment the counter (Lines 351-361).
    *   If the ID was manually edited or auto-filled, it calls `confirmID(idString, category)` (Lines 354-364). This ensures that if a user manually sets an ID to `S-500`, the next auto-generated one will be `S-501`.

#### **POS Billing View (`src/components/views/POSBillingView.tsx`)**
*   **Stable ID:** Upon mounting, the POS view generates a `rawInvoiceNo` using `peekNextID('sales')` (Line 135). This remains stable until the bill is saved.
*   **Display vs. Raw:** It distinguishes between a `rawInvoiceNo` (e.g., `S-101`) used as the database key and a `displayInvoiceNo` (e.g., `INV-0101`) formatted based on user settings (`invoice_prefix`) (Line 137).
*   **Persistence:** When the sale is finalized in `persist()`, it calls `confirmID(rawInvoiceNo, 'sales')` to officially advance the global counter (Line 392).

### Summary of Key Files
*   **`src/utils/idGenerator.ts`**: Contains the `PREFIX_MAP`, `generatePrefixedID`, `peekNextID`, and `seedCountersFromFirestore` logic.
*   **`src/components/modals/ManualEntryModal.tsx`**: Uses the generator for manual Sales, Purchases, and Transaction forms.
*   **`src/components/views/POSBillingView.tsx`**: Uses the generator for quick POS sales.
*   **`src/context/DataContext.tsx`**: Provides the data used to "seed" the counters from Firestore records.

Now I have the full picture. Here's the refined design before I implement:

**Behaviour contract:**
- **Auto-fill** → finds the first number ≥ `counter + 1` that doesn't already exist in the DB (gap-aware scan)
- **User manually edits** → that number is used; counter is set to that value (even if it's lower than current), becoming the new starting base for the next gap-scan
- **Next auto after manual edit** → scans forward from the manually-set number, skipping any that exist

**Example from the request:**
| Step | Action | Existing in DB | Suggested / Used | Counter after |
|------|---------|----------------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | auto | 101, 102, 105 | S-106 (skip 106? no → use 106) | 106 |
| 2 | user edits to 103 | 101, 102, 105 | S-103 used | **103** |
| 3 | auto | 101, 102, 103, 105 | 104 (104 free) | 104 |
| 4 | auto | 101, 102, 103, 104, 105 | 106 (105 exists → skip) | 106 |

---

**What changed in `idGenerator.ts`**

**1. `_existingNumbers` — new module-level store**
A `Map<category, Set<number>>` that holds every numeric part of every existing ID, populated when `seedCountersFromFirestore` is called (already called on data load). Updated on every `confirmID` and `generatePrefixedID` call so it stays live during the session.

**2. `findNextAvailable(start, existingSet)` — new internal helper**
Scans forward from `start` and returns the first number not in the set. Capped at 10 000 iterations to prevent infinite loops on pathological data.

**3. `peekNextID` + `generatePrefixedID` — gap-aware**
Both now call `findNextAvailable(counter + 1, existing)` instead of blindly returning `counter + 1`. If S-105 exists, the next auto-fill skips to S-106. If 104 is also free before 105, it returns 104 first.

**4. `confirmID` — always updates counter (even to a lower value)**
Previously only updated if the new number was higher. Now it always sets the counter to whatever was confirmed, and adds that number to the existing set. This is the key change that enables the manual-edit-then-continue flow:
- Auto-suggests S-106 → user types S-103 → saved → counter = 103
- Next peek: scans from 104, finds 104 free → S-104
- Next peek: scans from 105, 105 exists → skip → S-106 ✓

**5. `peekNextPartyCode` + `generatePartyCode` + `confirmPartyCode`** — same gap-aware logic applied to P-0001 style party codes.

No changes needed to `ManualEntryModal`, `POSBillingView`, or any other caller — the improvement is transparent since `seedCountersFromFirestore` already runs before any peek/generate call.
